mappings.rationale: Directly relevant to the cultural and literary context of Latin America during the independence period and early national development covered in this section.
Provides the colonial background of Spanish conquest essential to understanding the independence movements and the social structures (Creoles, mestizos, Indians) discussed in subsection A.1.
Directly covers the Mexican Revolutionary period (1910-17) including Huerta's coup, Carranza, Villa, Zapata, and the 1917 constitution discussed in subsection B.4.
Relevant to the literary and cultural developments across Latin American nations during and after the independence period covered in this section.
Provides essential background on indigenous Mexican civilization relevant to understanding the social dynamics of the Mexican independence movement and subsequent nation-building.
Relevant to the numerous revolutionary movements covered including the Latin American wars of independence, the Mexican War of Independence, and the Mexican Revolution.
Relevant to the Spanish and Portuguese colonial empires whose dissolution through independence movements is the central theme of this section.
Relevant to the postcolonial dynamics of nation-building, Creole identity, and the legacy of Spanish and Portuguese colonial rule throughout Latin America.
Relevant to the rise of national consciousness across Latin America driving the independence movements from Spain and Portugal discussed throughout this section.
The French Revolution is explicitly cited as a key influence on the Latin American independence movements in subsection A.1.a.
Relevant to the complex diplomatic relationships among Latin American nations and with European and U.S. powers throughout the independence era and after.
Relevant to the struggles between republican government, dictatorship, and democratic reform that characterize the political development of all Latin American nations in this section.
Relevant to the economic development patterns, foreign investment, and export economies (coffee, sugar, saltpetre) discussed across multiple Latin American nations.
Relevant to the numerous civil wars discussed including those in Mexico, Central America, Uruguay, Argentina, Chile, and the wars between Conservatives and Liberals throughout the region.
The entire section traces the decolonization of Latin America from Spanish, Portuguese, and French colonial rule through independence movements.
Napoleon's seizure of the Spanish throne and the Peninsular War (1808-14) are explicitly cited as catalysts for the Latin American independence movements in subsection A.1.c.
The Napoleonic Wars, particularly the Peninsular War in Spain, directly precipitated the Latin American independence movements described in subsection A.
Provides context for Brazilian development from the independent Empire under Pedro I (1822) through the First Republic discussed in subsection H.
Relevant to the emergence of Socialist parties in Chile (1901, 1912) and labor movements across Latin American nations discussed in subsections F.3 and others.
Provides context for the export-oriented economies (sugar, coffee, cotton, saltpetre, silver) and foreign investment patterns that shaped Latin American development.
